title: The doctrine of Total Depravity teaches that every faculty of fallen man — mind, will, conscience, and affections — is corrupted by sin such that he is wholly unable and unwilling to come to God apart from sovereign regenerating grace.
content:
# Dead in Trespasses: Why Total Depravity Is the Most Hopeful Doctrine You Will Ever Resist

There is a particular kind of Christian who comes to the doctrine of Total Depravity not with hostility but with unease. You have heard it taught. You do not deny Scripture. But something in you recoils — not at God, but at the apparent severity of the claim. If man cannot come to God, how can God hold him accountable for failing to come? This article is written for you. Not to corner you, but to walk with you through what Scripture actually teaches, and to show you that the doctrine you are resisting is the very doctrine that will one day move you to worship.

## What the Doctrine Actually Claims

Let us be precise, because imprecision here breeds the very confusion that troubles you.

Total Depravity teaches that every faculty of fallen man — mind, will, conscience, and affections — is corrupted by sin, such that he is wholly unable and unwilling to come to God apart from sovereign regenerating grace.

Notice the word *every*. This is not the claim that man is as wicked as he could possibly be, nor that he is incapable of acts of civic kindness. It is the claim that sin has reached into the whole man — that there is no clean room in the house, no faculty untouched, no chamber of the will that remains neutral and ready to choose God if only properly informed. The mind is darkened. The conscience is compromised. The affections are disordered. And the will — the seat of choosing — is corrupted along with the rest.

This is what makes the doctrine *total*: not the intensity of corruption but its extent. Sin has touched everything.

## The Scriptural Verdict

This is not a doctrine derived from theological deduction; it is a verdict pronounced by Scripture itself.

Consider Romans 3:10–12. Paul writes, "There is none righteous, not even one; there is none who understands, there is none who seeks for God." We must not read this as hyperbole — as preacher's exaggeration meant to humble us a bit. It is a judicial verdict. It is God's courtroom assessment of the human race apart from grace. *None* seeks God. Not a few. Not most. None.

If you find that statement hard to swallow, recognize that this is precisely the point at which the doctrine of Total Depravity is being tested in your own heart. The text leaves no room for a remnant of self-seeking sinners groping their way toward God.

Ephesians 2:1–3 deepens the diagnosis. The unregenerate are not sick; they are "dead in trespasses and sins." They walk "according to the course of this world." They are "by nature children of wrath." Mark that phrase — *by nature*. This is not a wrath earned by isolated acts but a condition of birth, a corruption inherited and inhabited. A dead man does not improve. He does not reach. He does not cooperate. He is dead.

And then John 6:44, where the Lord Himself settles the question of ability: "No one is able to come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws him." Ability itself — the very capacity to come to Christ — is a gift. It is granted by the drawing of the Father. It does not reside in the sinner as a latent power waiting to be exercised. It must be given.

These three texts together form the spine of the doctrine: none seeks (Romans 3), because all are dead (Ephesians 2), and no one can come unless drawn (John 6).

## The Objection You Are Actually Asking

Now to the heart of your unease. The objection has an ancient pedigree and a deceptive logic: *ought implies can*. If God commands men to repent, surely men must possess the ability to repent. Otherwise God commands the impossible and then condemns men for failing — which seems monstrous.

The answer turns on a distinction that, once seen, resolves the entire difficulty: the difference between **natural inability** and **moral inability**.

Total Depravity does not teach that man has lost his natural faculties. He still possesses a will — he chooses constantly. He still possesses reason — he thinks, deliberates, weighs. He still possesses a conscience — he knows the difference between right and wrong and feels the weight of guilt. Nothing in the apparatus of human agency has been destroyed. The man who refuses Christ is fully equipped, as a creature, to choose.

What has been corrupted is not the machinery but the disposition. His will is enslaved to corrupt desires. He does not come to Christ — and here is the entire matter — *because he does not want to*. His inability is not the inability of a man chained against his will, longing to come but prevented. It is the inability of a man whose wanting itself is bent away from God.

This is why the objection fails. God does not command a man to do what his nature makes impossible in the way a man might be commanded to fly. He commands a man to do what that man refuses to do. And that refusal — that settled, willing, native unwillingness — is not an excuse. *It is the condemnation.* The sinner is not innocent because he cannot come; he is guilty precisely because he will not, and his "cannot" is only the measure of how thoroughly he "will not."

You asked how a good God could hold men responsible for what they cannot do. The answer is that the cannot is itself a will-not, and a man is always responsible for what he will not do when what he refuses is God Himself.

## Why This Ends in Worship

If you have followed the argument this far with honesty, you may feel its weight pressing down. Good. That weight is doing its work. Because the doctrine that humbles is the doctrine that frees, and Total Depravity does not terminate in despair — it terminates in doxology.

Here is why. If man is wholly unable and unwilling to come to God apart from sovereign regenerating grace, then every ground of human boasting is stripped away. There is no room for the testimony that begins, "I was the one who finally chose God." The man who truly understands Total Depravity cannot say it. What he says instead is what Paul said: "By the grace of God I am what I am" (1 Corinthians 15:10).

Do you see what this doctrine has done? It has taken salvation entirely out of your hands and laid it at the feet of sovereign grace. And what you lose in self-congratulation, you gain a hundredfold in wonder. For now the question is no longer, "Why would God condemn a sinner who could not come?" The question becomes the only question that ever mattered: *Why would God regenerate any sinner at all?*

That He should reach into the graveyard of Ephesians 2 and breathe life into dead men. That He should draw, by the John 6 power of the Father, those who would never have come on their own. That He should make willing the unwilling, and seeking the un-seeking — this is not severity. This is mercy beyond accounting.

So the proper response of the man who grasps Total Depravity is not argument, and not anxiety, but worship. Gratitude, that grace found you when you would never have sought it. Humility, that you contributed nothing but the deadness from which you were raised. And wonder — sheer, unending wonder — that the God whose verdict over the whole race was "none seeks" should sovereignly create seekers out of the very dust of those who would not.

To Him alone, who saves dead men who could not and would not save themselves, be all glory, honor, and praise. Amen.

answers.q1: The doctrine of Total Depravity teaches that every faculty of fallen man — mind, will, conscience, and affections — is corrupted by sin such that he is wholly unable and unwilling to come to God apart from sovereign regenerating grace.
answers.q2: Romans 3:10-12 establishes that none seeks God — not as hyperbole but as judicial verdict on the human condition. Ephesians 2:1-3 teaches that the unregenerate are dead in trespasses, children of wrath by nature, following the course of this world. John 6:44 confirms that no one is able to come to Christ unless the Father draws him — ability itself is a gift of grace.
answers.q3: A thoughtful Christian who has heard the doctrine of Total Depravity and finds it troubling — not an enemy of the faith but someone who genuinely wrestles with how a good God could hold men responsible for what they cannot do. The tone should be serious but pastoral — not combative.
answers.q4: The objection is "ought implies can" — if God commands repentance, man must have the ability to comply. The answer is that Total Depravity produces moral inability not natural inability. Man possesses every natural faculty — will, reason, conscience — but his will is enslaved to corrupt desires. He will not come because he does not want to, and that unwillingness is itself the condemnation.
answers.q5: This doctrine ends in worship because it strips every ground of human boasting and lays salvation entirely at the feet of sovereign grace. The man who understands Total Depravity does not say "I chose God" — he says with Paul, "by the grace of God I am what I am." The proper response is gratitude, humility, and wonder that God would regenerate any sinner at all.
